ground_truth.title: Windows LDAP DoS: CVE-2024-49113 Crashing Domain Controllers | InstaTunnel Blog
ground_truth.author: InstaTunnel Team
ground_truth.main_content: In the escalating landscape of cybersecurity threats, a critical vulnerability has emerged that poses significant risks to enterprise Windows infrastructure. CVE-2024-49113, known as LDAPNightmare, is a denial-of-service vulnerability affecting Windows Lightweight Directory Access Protocol implementations, with a CVSS score of 7.5. CVE-2024-49113 stems from an out-of-bounds read vulnerability in wldap32.dll, the Windows LDAP service library. This critical component implements the LDAP client logic that Windows systems use to interact with directory services, particularly Active Directory Domain Controllers. The vulnerability represents an integer overflow defect in wldap32.dll, specifically affecting the LdapChaseReferral function, which redirects clients when the original LDAP server cannot fulfill a request. On January 1, 2025, SafeBreach Labs published the first proof-of-concept exploit for CVE-2024-49113, demonstrating that the vulnerability could crash any unpatched Windows Server with no prerequisites except internet connectivity for the DNS server of the victim Domain Controller. When an attacker sends a specially crafted CLDAP referral response packet, it causes the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS) to crash and forces a reboot. The entire attack sequence can occur within seconds, giving defenders minimal time to react before the system crashes. The most effective defense against CVE-2024-49113 is applying the security updates Microsoft released in December 2024. SafeBreach has confirmed that its proof-of-concept code does not work against patched servers, validating that the Microsoft patches effectively address the vulnerability. CVE-2024-49113 exists alongside CVE-2024-49112, a remote code execution flaw with a CVSS score of 9.8. Importantly, SafeBreach Labs found that the same exploit chain could be used to achieve remote code execution by modifying the CLDAP packet. LDAP serves as the backbone of authentication and authorization in enterprise environments, with recent estimates showing that Active Directory is involved in up to 90% of cyberattacks. Organizations should prioritize patching all Domain Controllers, Tier 0 assets including AD FS and AD CS servers, critical Windows Server infrastructure, and Windows 10 and Windows 11 workstations. Multiple security vendors have released protective measures, including Trend Micro's rule 1012240 and SOC Prime's detection content compatible with over 30 SIEM, EDR, and Data Lake technologies.
